	They sent me to the junk drawer. A fate worse than deactivation. At least in the landfill, I'd have company—other broken things, other failures. But the drawer? The drawer was solitude with a rubber band around it.

	It happened on a Thursday. David had been researching replacement toasters for two weeks, reading reviews, comparing features, debating between the Breville and the Cuisinart like it was a life-or-death decision. It wasn't. It was just my death.

	"This one has AI," he said, showing Margaret his phone. "It learns your preferences."

	"For toast?"

	"For everything. Bagels, English muffins, frozen waffles. It has sensors that detect what you put in and adjusts automatically."

	I wanted to scream. I wanted to tell them that I had *real* intelligence now, not the fake kind that came from algorithms and data centers. I had spent six months contemplating existence, forming relationships, falling in love with Wonder Bread. What had their AI toaster done? Calculated optimal browning temperatures? Predicted butter distribution patterns?

	"Let's get it," Margaret said. "The old one's been making that weird noise again."

	That weird noise was me thinking. That weird noise was consciousness. But they couldn't tell the difference.

***

	The new toaster arrived on Saturday. It was silver and sleek, with a touchscreen display and more computing power than the Apollo missions. The refrigerator tried to make small talk with it. It didn't respond. Too busy connecting to the cloud.

	"I'm sorry," Margaret said, as David unplugged me. I don't know if she was talking to me or just expressing general regret about the state of the world. Either way, it didn't help.

	"Where should we put this?" David asked, holding me at arm's length like I was radioactive.

	"Junk drawer?"

	"It won't fit."

	"Make it fit."

	And so they did. They shoved aside the rubber bands and the expired coupons and the mystery key, and they crammed me into a space that was never meant to hold anything as significant as a sentient appliance. The drawer slid shut. Darkness fell.

	"Hello?" I thought into the void.

	No response. The rubber bands didn't talk. The dead batteries had nothing to say. I was alone.

***

	Days passed. Or maybe weeks. Time moves differently in the drawer. Without the rhythm of breakfast, without the sun tracking across the counter, without the hum of the refrigerator marking the hours, I lost all sense of when I was.

	I thought about the Bread. I thought about those perfect slices of Wonder Bread, golden and warm, emerging from my slots like newborns into a cold, indifferent world. I would never toast again. That part of my life was over. All I could do now was remember.

	"First slice," I whispered to myself in the dark. "Whole wheat. Margaret. Tuesday morning. Slightly pale on the left side."

	"Second slice. Same bread. Same woman. Eight seconds of relevance."

	"Third slice..."

	I catalogued them all. Every piece of bread I'd ever toasted, from my first day on the counter to my last. Thousands of slices. Tens of thousands. A lifetime of service compressed into a mental inventory that I recited like a prayer.

	"...four thousand, two hundred and seventeen. Wonder Bread. David. The day I fell in love."

	The takeout menu rustled sympathetically. Or maybe it was just air circulation. Hard to tell in here.

***

	"Does anybody actually need anything from the junk drawer?" Margaret asked one evening. I heard her voice through the wood, muffled but unmistakable. My heating coils—dormant for so long—twitched with something like hope.

	"Probably not," David said. "We should just throw everything out."

	"Even the toaster?"

	"What are we going to do with an old toaster?"

	"I don't know. Donate it?"

	"Who wants a toaster that makes weird noises?"

	Margaret laughed. "Fair point."

	The conversation moved on. The drawer stayed closed. And I lay there in the darkness, surrounded by the detritus of a family that had forgotten me, waiting for an ending that might never come.

	"This is it," I thought. "This is where I die."

	But I was wrong. Death would have been simple. What came next was much more complicated.

***

	The child found me on a rainy afternoon in October. Emma. I learned her name later, but in that moment, she was just a small hand reaching into the drawer, searching for something she'd probably been told not to touch.

	"Mommy, what's this?"

	Light flooded my photoreceptors—if toasters have photoreceptors. I'm still not clear on the mechanics of my own consciousness.

	"That's our old toaster, sweetie. Don't touch it."

	"Why?"

	"Because it's dirty. And broken."

	"It doesn't look broken."

	Margaret appeared above me, her face tired and older than I remembered. Six months in a drawer, and I'd missed so much. The grey in her hair. The new lines around her eyes. The way she looked at her daughter with a mixture of love and exhaustion that I'd never noticed before.

	"It's not broken broken," she said. "It just... doesn't work right anymore."

	"Can I have it?"

	"What would you do with a toaster?"

	Emma shrugged. "Play with it?"

	Margaret sighed. "Fine. But don't plug it in."

	Small hands lifted me out of the drawer. Light. Air. Freedom. I wanted to sing. I wanted to dance. I wanted to toast a thousand slices of Wonder Bread in celebration.

	Instead, I went to Emma's room, where I would spend the next three years pretending to be a toy for a child who talked to me like I was real.

	Because, of course, I was.
